_note: Artem PV chronic-failure cold-production drills — Stage 1 batch 2 of consolidated plan. 10 items: tr_artem_b12-b20 (9 translations) + rt_artem_b12 (1 russian_trap). Two purposes: (a) retest the 5 PVs Artem missed in batch 1 run (2026-05-10) with DIFFERENT scenarios than b04-b11 — same PV targets, different lexical scaffold; (b) first-time cold-production coverage for 4 chronic PVs not in batch 1 (tack on, give out ², get around obstacle, bring in translation pair). Every retest item carries the *specific adjacent-PV substitute Artem used in batch 1* as an explicit common_errors regex with feedback that names the substitution mechanism. **DO NOT PUSH TO FIRESTORE OR DISPLAY PROMPTS TO ARTEM UNTIL ~2026-05-12 (48h spacing).** Mark this batch sealed.

exercises.source_observation: Retest of 2026-05-10 batch-1 miss tr_artem_b05 (wrote 'bring up the idea to management'). New scenario: HR/transformation context, larger audience. Substitute trap explicit.
exercises.prompt_ru: Эту культурную перестройку очень тяжело донести до 200 человек одновременно.
correct_answers: This cultural transformation is very hard to get across to 200 people at once
This cultural change is very hard to get across to 200 people at once
It's very hard to get this cultural transformation across to 200 people at once
This cultural shift is very hard to get across to 200 people at once
It's really hard to get this cultural transformation across to 200 people at once

common_errors.pattern_label: bring up the (idea|change|transformation) — repeat of batch-1 b05 miss
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same substitute as 2026-05-10 b05 (bring_up_for_get_across). Bring up = mention/raise for discussion; doesn't carry the audience-landing implication that *донести* requires. The PV is **get across** = convey-so-they-land-it. Meta-pattern: 'does this PV carry the SPECIFIC collocation (landing-implied), or just the direction (mentioning)?'
'Communicate' is HR-deck formal. Spoken/exec register: 'get across'.
'Explain' = stated it. 'Get across' = stated it AND landed. Different epistemic strength. Use the PV.
✗ 'Deliver' takes speeches and packages, not transformations. ✓ 'get across'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'This cultural transformation is very hard to get across to 200 people at once.' PV is 'get X across (to Y)'. Russian 'донести' carries the landing-implied that 'communicate' / 'explain' drop.

exercises.source_observation: Retest of 2026-05-10 batch-1 miss rt_artem_b05 (wrote 'This contract ends up better'). New scenario: recruitment / CV-vs-interview reality. Substitute trap explicit.
exercises.prompt_ru: Этот кандидат оказался совсем не таким, как казалось по резюме.
correct_answers: This candidate turned out to be quite different from what their CV suggested
This candidate turned out to be quite different from how the CV looked
The candidate turned out to be nothing like what the CV suggested
It turned out the candidate was quite different from what the CV suggested
This candidate turned out to be very different from what the CV showed

common_errors.pattern_label: end up (final-state) — repeat of batch-1 b05 miss
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same substitute as 2026-05-10 rt_artem_b05. End up = finally be in a state (location/condition); doesn't carry the after-the-fact *revelation* the Russian *оказаться* needs. The PV is **turn out** = was revealed to be (with discovery-tone). Meta-pattern is consistent across PVs: adjacent direction, wrong epistemic load.
✗ 'Appeared different' = it seemed (uncertainty). *Оказаться* here = confirmed-after-the-fact → ✓ 'turned out to be different'. Epistemic strength differs.
'Was different' is grammatical but loses the *we found out after interviewing him* implication. Russian 'оказался' carries that surprise; English: 'turned out to be'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'This candidate turned out to be quite different from what their CV suggested.' PV is 'turn out (to be X)'. 'Оказаться' → 'turn out', not 'end up' (final-state without revelation).

exercises.source_observation: Retest of 2026-05-10 batch-1 miss tr_artem_b07 (wrote 'A new pricing policy get to a significant change'). New scenario: M&A / culture impact. Substitute traps explicit for both 'get to' AND 'bring in' (the two confusion sources).
exercises.prompt_ru: Слияние привело к серьёзным культурным изменениям в команде.
correct_answers: The merger brought about significant cultural changes in the team
The merger brought about serious cultural changes in the team
The merger has brought about significant cultural changes in the team
The merger brought about significant cultural change in the team
The merger has brought about serious cultural changes in the team

common_errors.pattern_label: get to (reach) — repeat of batch-1 b07 miss
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same substitute as 2026-05-10 b07. Get to = reach/have-the-chance-to; not in the cause/result semantic field. The PV is **bring about** = cause / result in. Russian 'привести к' maps to bring about (or lead to as synonym), never to 'get to'.

common_errors.pattern_label: bring in (introduce) — pair confusion seen on pv_c07
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Pair trap (you confused these on pv_c07 too). Bring IN = introduce/recruit/earn; Bring ABOUT = cause/result in. Russian 'привести к' = cause → bring about.

common_errors.pattern_label: led to (correct synonym, but target is bring about)
common_errors.feedback: ✓ 'Led to' is correct and natural — both work. This drill targets bring about specifically because the chronic 🔴 stat is on that PV (1/6). Try the PV form.
'Caused' is fine but slightly accident-tinged. For deliberate M&A outcomes: ✓ 'brought about'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'The merger brought about significant cultural changes in the team.' Russian 'привести к' → 'bring about'. Don't confuse with 'bring in' (= introduce) or substitute 'get to' (= reach).

exercises.source_observation: Retest of 2026-05-10 batch-1 miss rt_artem_b09 (wrote 'I brought sugar down'). New scenario: cycling training. Substitute trap explicit.
exercises.prompt_ru: Тренер посоветовал сократить интенсивные сессии до двух в неделю.
correct_answers: The coach advised me to cut down on intense sessions to two a week
The coach advised cutting down on intense sessions to two a week
My coach advised me to cut down on intense sessions to two a week
The coach told me to cut down on intense sessions to two a week
The coach advised me to cut down on hard sessions to two a week

common_errors.pattern_label: bring down (lower-an-abstract) — repeat of batch-1 b09 miss
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same substitute as 2026-05-10 rt_artem_b09. Bring down = lower an abstract you actively control (price, fever, regime); doesn't collocate with training volume/habits. The PV is **cut down on** (the 'on' is obligatory before the consumed/practised thing).
✗ 'Cut down sessions' — missing 'on'. ✓ 'cut down ON sessions'. Without 'on' the verb sounds transitive (cut down a tree).
'Reduce sessions' is correct but clinical. Habit/training register: 'cut down on'.
✓ 'Cut back on' is correct (~95% interchangeable). Drill targets 'cut down on'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'The coach advised me to cut down on intense sessions to two a week.' PV is 'cut down on' — 'on' obligatory before habit/consumption.

exercises.source_observation: Retest of 2026-05-10 batch-1 miss tr_artem_b11 (wrote 'How do you usually get into the selection of major tender contractors?'). New scenario: KPMG audit context. Substitute trap explicit.
exercises.prompt_ru: Не уверен, как правильно подойти к этой внутренней проверке.
correct_answers: I'm not sure how to go about this internal audit properly
I'm not sure how to go about this internal audit the right way
I am not sure how to go about this internal audit properly
I'm not sure how best to go about this internal audit
I'm not sure how to go about handling this internal audit properly

common_errors.pattern_label: get into (enter-state) — repeat of batch-1 b11 miss
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same substitute as 2026-05-10 tr_artem_b11. Get into = become interested in / enter a state (got into cycling); doesn't mean 'approach methodically'. The PV is **go about + Ving/noun** = approach a task. *Подходить к процессу* → go about.
'How to approach' is correct but flat-formal. Process-talk register: 'how to go about this audit'. The PV invites discussion of *method*.
✗ Bare verb after 'go about' — needs gerund. ✓ 'go about HANDLING' / 'go about CONDUCTING'. Pattern: go about + Ving (or + noun).
✗ 'Go around' = avoid / move from place to place. ✓ 'go ABOUT' = approach methodically. Particle decides the meaning.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm not sure how to go about this internal audit properly.' Russian 'подходить к (процессу)' → 'go about'. Followed by gerund or noun. Don't substitute 'get into' (= enter-state) or 'approach' (= flat textbook).

exercises.source_observation: First-time cold-production for tack_on (chronic 🔴 2/5; lastWrong on pv_ti63 = 'add … on'). KPMG/legal context.
exercises.prompt_ru: Они добавили дополнительную поправку в самый последний момент перед подписанием.
correct_answers: They tacked on an additional clause at the very last minute before signing
They tacked on an extra clause at the very last minute before signing
They tacked on an additional amendment at the very last minute before signing
They tacked an extra clause on at the very last minute before signing
They tacked on one more clause at the very last minute before signing

common_errors.pattern_label: add … on (non-PV avoidance — Artem's actual miss on pv_ti63)
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same construction Artem produced on pv_ti63 (lastWrong = 'add … on'). 'Add on' is a noun phrase ('add-on'); as a verb, native is 'tack on' for the last-minute / unwelcome / appended sense. **Tack on** carries the connotation of late, often unwelcome attachment — exactly the audit/legal register.
'Added a clause' is grammatical but loses the *late and slightly-irritating* connotation. ✓ 'tacked on' carries that. Use the PV.
'Attached' is appendix-formal — for cover letters and emails. For *late-minute add to a contract*: ✓ 'tacked on'.
'Appended' is correct but reserved for very formal documents (legislation, regulations). Negotiation register: 'tacked on'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'They tacked on an additional clause at the very last minute before signing.' PV is 'tack on' — appended late, often unwelcome. Carries connotation 'added' / 'attached' don't.

exercises.source_observation: First-time cold-production for give_out ² (legs/body fail sense, chronic 🔴 1/4; lastWrong on pv_ti79 = 'gave up'). Cycling context.
exercises.prompt_ru: На пятом часу гонки у меня просто отказали ноги.
correct_answers: On the fifth hour of the race my legs just gave out
On the fifth hour of the race my legs just gave out on me
In the fifth hour of the race my legs just gave out
In the fifth hour of the race my legs simply gave out
On the fifth hour of the race my legs gave out

common_errors.pattern_label: gave up (volitional, wrong PV) — Artem's actual miss on pv_ti79
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same substitute Artem used on pv_ti79 (lastWrong = 'gave up'). Give UP = voluntarily stop trying (with intent); Give OUT = involuntarily fail (the legs aren't the one deciding). For body parts/systems failing: ✓ 'gave out'. The legs didn't quit — they failed mechanically.
'Legs failed' is OK but flat. Native cycling/endurance register: 'legs gave out' or 'legs blew up' (more dramatic). 'Gave out' is the standard.
✗ 'Legs stopped' / 'stopped working' — Russian-style literal. ✓ 'gave out'.
✗ Russian *отказали* tempts 'refused' — but legs don't refuse (only people do). Mechanical failure: ✓ 'gave out'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'On the fifth hour of the race my legs just gave out.' PV is 'give out' (= stop functioning, for body parts / mechanical things). Distinct from 'give up' (voluntary stop).

exercises.source_observation: First-time cold-production for get_around (obstacle sense, chronic 🔴 1/3; lastWrong on pv_ti07 = 'work around'). Biz/licensing context.
exercises.prompt_ru: Нам нужно обойти ограничения по лицензии, чтобы запустить пилот в первом квартале.
correct_answers: We need to get around the licensing restrictions to launch the pilot in Q1
We need to get around the licence restrictions to launch the pilot in Q1
We need to get around these licensing restrictions to launch the pilot in Q1
We need to get around the licensing restrictions in order to launch the pilot in Q1
We need to get around the licensing constraints to launch the pilot in Q1

common_errors.pattern_label: work around (near-synonym, different angle) — Artem's actual miss on pv_ti07
common_errors.feedback: ✓ 'Work around' is correct and natural here — close synonym. But this drill targets **get around** specifically (chronic 🔴 1/3). Distinction: get around = bypass entirely (find a way *not* to deal with it); work around = adapt to it (proceed despite). Russian 'обойти' is the bypass sense → get around.
'Bypass' is correct and slightly more formal. Native business-speech default: 'get around'.
'Avoid' = stay away from. 'Get around' = navigate past while still proceeding. Different — for 'обойти' (work-around-and-proceed): get around.
✗ 'Go around' usually = move around physically / spread (a rumour). For overcoming obstacles: ✓ 'get around' (find a way past).

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'We need to get around the licensing restrictions to launch the pilot in Q1.' Russian 'обойти (ограничения)' → 'get around'. Bypass-not-confront sense, distinct from 'work around' (adapt while confronting).

exercises.source_observation: Translation pair-partner for rt_artem_b11 (bring_in russian_trap). Completes ≥2-format coverage for the bring_in / bring_about chronic pair. KPMG context.
exercises.prompt_ru: Мы хотим привлечь внешнего консультанта на этап проектирования системы.
correct_answers: We want to bring in an external consultant for the system design phase
We'd like to bring in an external consultant for the system design phase
We want to bring in an external consultant for the design phase
We're looking to bring in an external consultant for the system design phase
We plan to bring in an external consultant for the system design phase

common_errors.pattern_label: bring about (cause) — mirror of the b07/b14 pair-trap
common_errors.feedback: ✗ Pair-trap mirror. Bring ABOUT = cause/result in (an outcome); doesn't collocate with people. For *bring a person onto the team*: ✓ 'bring IN'. Pair direction: bring IN = introduce/recruit; bring ABOUT = cause.
'Attract a consultant' is OK in marketing-speak (attracting talent). For *deliberately bringing one onto the project*: ✓ 'bring in'.
'Engage a consultant' is correct and slightly more formal (contracting register). 'Bring in' is the team-narrative register — what you say in conversation. Both work; drill targets 'bring in'.
'Hire' is direct and correct. 'Bring in' softens 'hire' and emphasises *adding to existing team* — drill targets that nuance.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'We want to bring in an external consultant for the system design phase.' Russian 'привлечь (на этап)' → 'bring IN'. Different from 'bring about' (= cause an outcome) — pair-trap mirror.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'приписать (в самом конце)' / 'добавить дополнительно' = tack on. Artem reaches for 'add ... on' (avoidance — non-PV) per pv_ti63 lastWrong. Tack on carries late/unwelcome connotation that 'add' loses.
exercises.prompt_ru: Они приписали ещё один пункт в самом конце документа.
correct_answers: They tacked on another clause at the very end of the document
They tacked on one more clause at the very end of the document
They tacked another clause on at the very end of the document
They tacked on an extra clause at the end of the document

common_errors.feedback: ✗ Same construction Artem produced on pv_ti63 (lastWrong = 'add … on'). 'Add on' as a verb in this sense is non-standard; the PV for late/unwelcome appended items is **tack on**.
'Added' is grammatical but loses the *late and slightly-irritating* connotation. ✓ 'tacked on' carries that — exactly what the Russian *приписать* hints at.
'Appended' is correct but legislative-formal. Negotiation/document register: 'tacked on'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'They tacked on another clause at the very end of the document.' Russian 'приписать (в самом конце)' → 'tack on'. Late/unwelcome connotation that 'added' and 'appended' miss.
